paper_title: Independent Cr2O3 functions as efficient cocatalyst on the crystal facets engineered TiO2 for photocatalytic CO2 reduction

co-catalyst.source: Independent Cr2O3 functions as efficient cocatalyst on the crystal facets engineered TiO2 for photocatalytic CO2 reduction
co-catalyst.context: Independent Cr2O3 functions as efficient cocatalyst on the crystal facets engineered TiO2 for photocatalytic CO2 reduction

co-catalyst.llm generation: Cr2O3 is used as a cocatalyst on crystal facets engineered TiO2 for photocatalytic CO2 reduction.
co-catalyst.source: In the present work, we construct independent Cr 2 O 3 as an efficient cocatalyst on the crystal facets engineered TiO 2 for photocatalytic CO 2 reduction for the first time.
co-catalyst.context: Implanting cocatalysts on semiconductor photocatalysts is considered as an essential strategy to promote reaction activity.However, the development of highly efficient cocatalysts remains challenging.In the present work, we construct independent Cr 2 O 3 as an efficient cocatalyst on the crystal facets engineered TiO 2 for photocatalytic CO 2 reduction for the first time.Moreover, a combined strategy of crystal facets engineering of TiO 2 and loading of Cr 2 O 3 cocatalyst is adopted to further promote the charge carriers' separation/transfer.In comparison with the cocatalyst-free TiO 2 without facets engineering, 2HF-TiO 2 /0.2Cr 2 O 3 exhibits near 30-fold enhancement on the CO 2 conversion efficiency.The enhanced photocatalytic activity is attributed to the maximal synergy of crystal facets engineering and cocatalyst loading for well-matched CO 2 reduction half-reaction and H 2 O oxidation halfreaction.These findings not only provide new insights for understanding the role of Cr 2 O 3 in photocatalytic systems but also may shed light on the design of photocatalysts from the perspective of overall photocatalytic CO 2 reduction.

co-catalyst.source: In summary, independent Cr 2 O 3 has been uncovered as an efficient cocatalysts on crystal facets engineered TiO 2 for photocatalytic CO reduction for the first time.
co-catalyst.context: In summary, independent Cr 2 O 3 has been uncovered as an efficient cocatalysts on crystal facets engineered TiO 2 for photocatalytic CO reduction for the first time.Through adopting the combined strategy of crystal facets engineering and cocatalyst loading, the photocatalytic CO reduction activity over the optimized photocatalyst (2HF-TiO 2 / 0.2Cr 2 O 3 ) was promoted near 30 times in comparison with that over cocatalyst-free TiO 2 without facets engineering (0HT-TiO 2 ).The superior photocatalytic performance was attributed to the maximal synergy of loading Cr 2 O 3 cocatalyst and TiO 2 crystal facets engineering.It was revealed that the maximal synergy dramatically facilitated the photogenerated electrons' transfer from TiO 2 to Cr 2 O 3 , endowing more photogenerated electrons and holes to participate in the following wellmatched CO 2 reduction half-reaction and H 2 O oxidation half-reaction.This study may not only provide inspiration for the deepened understanding of the role of Cr 2 O 3 in photocatalytic systems but also shed light on the design of highly-efficient photocatalysts. Fig. 1.(a) XRD patterns of 3HF-TiO 2 /0.1Cr 2 O 3 and 3HF-TiO 2 ; (b) High resolution XPS in Cr 2p region of 3HF-TiO 2 /0.1Cr 2 O 3 ; (c) High resolution XPS in Ti 2p region of 3HF-TiO 2 /0.1Cr 2 O 3 and 3HF-TiO 2 ; (d) High resolution XPS in O 1s region of 3HF-TiO 2 /0.1Cr 2 O 3 and 3HF-TiO 2 . Fig. 2. (a)(c) HRTEM images of 3HF-TiO 2 /0.1Cr 2 O 3 and 3HF-TiO 2 , respectively; (b)(d) the magnificent images of the yellow box area in (a) and (c), respectively; (e) HAADF image of 3HF-TiO 2 /0.1Cr 2 O 3 ; (f)(g)(h) the corresponding EDS mapping of Cr, Ti, and O, respectively.(For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.) Fig. 3. (a) The photocatalytic activity of CO 2 reduction over 3HF-TiO 2 /yCr 2 O 3 (y 0, 0.05, 0.1, and 0.5).Pure Cr 2 O 3 powders, no photocatalyst and no irradiation were conducted as control experiments.The inset is the SVUV-PIMS spectrum of the products after 13 CO 2 photoreduction over 3HF-TiO 2 /0.1Cr 2 O 3 ; (b) UV-Vis DRS of 3HF-TiO 2 and 3HF-TiO 2 /0.1Cr 2 O 3 ; (c) PL spectra of 3HF-TiO 2 and 3HF-TiO 2 /0.1Cr 2 O 3 . Dong: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation.Zhiyu Wang: Investigation, Validation.Heng Cao: Investigation, Validation.Jiawei Xue: Resources, Writing -review editing.Chengyuan Liu: Resources, Formal analysis.Song Sun: Writing -review editing, Funding acquisition.Chen Gao: Writing -review editing, Funding acquisition.Xiaodi Zhu: Supervision, Data curation, Writing -original draft, Funding acquisition.Jun Bao: Supervision, Writing -review Fig. 4. (a)(b)(c) HRTEM images of 0HF-TiO 2 /0.5Cr 2 O 3 , 1HF-TiO 2 /0.3Cr 2 O 3 and 2HF-TiO 2 /0.2Cr 2 O 3 , respectively; (d) The photocatalytic activity of CO 2 reduction over 0HF-TiO 2 /0.5Cr 2 O 3 , 1HF-TiO 2 /0.3Cr 2 O 3 , 2HF-TiO 2 /0.2Cr 2 O 3 and 3HF-TiO 2 /0.1Cr 2 O 3 ; (e) The comparison of photocatalytic activity among 0HF-TiO 2 (TiO modulated with neither facets engineering nor cocatalysts loading), 3HF-TiO 2 (TiO 2 singly modulated with crystal facets engineering), 0HF-TiO 2 /0.5Cr 2 O 3 (TiO singly modulated with the optimal cocatalysts loading) and 2HF-TiO 2 /0.2Cr 2 O 3 (TiO 2 co-modulated with crystal facets engineering and cocatalysts loading). Fig. 5. (a) The LSV curves in 1 M NaOH solution under 300 W Xe lamp illumination (100 mW/cm 2 ); (b) Amperometric I-t curves under chopped light irradiation with on/off interval of 30 s at 1.23 V vs. RHE; (c) EIS spectra; and (d) The time-resolved PL spectra of 0HF-TiO 2 , 2HF-TiO 2 , 3HF-TiO 2 , 0HF-TiO 2 /0.5Cr 2 O 3 and 2HF-TiO 2 /0.2Cr 2 O 3 . This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2016YFB0700205, 2017YFA0403402, 2019YFA0405602), National Natural Science Foundation of China (52002367, 21673214, U1632273, U1732272, U1832165), the foundation from Users with Excellence Program of Hefei Science Center CAS (2020HSC-UE001) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (WK2310000093).We also thank the support supports from the beamline BL10B and beamline BL03U at the National Synchrotron Radiation Laboratory (NSRL) of the University of Science and Technology of China. The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2021.149634.

light source.context: In summary, independent Cr 2 O 3 has been uncovered as an efficient cocatalysts on crystal facets engineered TiO 2 for photocatalytic CO reduction for the first time.Through adopting the combined strategy of crystal facets engineering and cocatalyst loading, the photocatalytic CO reduction activity over the optimized photocatalyst (2HF-TiO 2 / 0.2Cr 2 O 3 ) was promoted near 30 times in comparison with that over cocatalyst-free TiO 2 without facets engineering (0HT-TiO 2 ).The superior photocatalytic performance was attributed to the maximal synergy of loading Cr 2 O 3 cocatalyst and TiO 2 crystal facets engineering.It was revealed that the maximal synergy dramatically facilitated the photogenerated electrons' transfer from TiO 2 to Cr 2 O 3 , endowing more photogenerated electrons and holes to participate in the following wellmatched CO 2 reduction half-reaction and H 2 O oxidation half-reaction.This study may not only provide inspiration for the deepened understanding of the role of Cr 2 O 3 in photocatalytic systems but also shed light on the design of highly-efficient photocatalysts. Fig. 1.(a) XRD patterns of 3HF-TiO 2 /0.1Cr 2 O 3 and 3HF-TiO 2 ; (b) High resolution XPS in Cr 2p region of 3HF-TiO 2 /0.1Cr 2 O 3 ; (c) High resolution XPS in Ti 2p region of 3HF-TiO 2 /0.1Cr 2 O 3 and 3HF-TiO 2 ; (d) High resolution XPS in O 1s region of 3HF-TiO 2 /0.1Cr 2 O 3 and 3HF-TiO 2 . Fig. 2. (a)(c) HRTEM images of 3HF-TiO 2 /0.1Cr 2 O 3 and 3HF-TiO 2 , respectively; (b)(d) the magnificent images of the yellow box area in (a) and (c), respectively; (e) HAADF image of 3HF-TiO 2 /0.1Cr 2 O 3 ; (f)(g)(h) the corresponding EDS mapping of Cr, Ti, and O, respectively.(For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.) Fig. 3. (a) The photocatalytic activity of CO 2 reduction over 3HF-TiO 2 /yCr 2 O 3 (y 0, 0.05, 0.1, and 0.5).Pure Cr 2 O 3 powders, no photocatalyst and no irradiation were conducted as control experiments.The inset is the SVUV-PIMS spectrum of the products after 13 CO 2 photoreduction over 3HF-TiO 2 /0.1Cr 2 O 3 ; (b) UV-Vis DRS of 3HF-TiO 2 and 3HF-TiO 2 /0.1Cr 2 O 3 ; (c) PL spectra of 3HF-TiO 2 and 3HF-TiO 2 /0.1Cr 2 O 3 . (a)(b)(c) HRTEM images of 0HF-TiO 2 /0.5Cr 2 O 3 , 1HF-TiO 2 /0.3Cr 2 O 3 and 2HF-TiO 2 /0.2Cr 2 O 3 , respectively; (d) The photocatalytic activity of CO 2 reduction over 0HF-TiO 2 /0.5Cr 2 O 3 , 1HF-TiO 2 /0.3Cr 2 O 3 , 2HF-TiO 2 /0.2Cr 2 O 3 and 3HF-TiO 2 /0.1Cr 2 O 3 ; (e) The comparison of photocatalytic activity among 0HF-TiO 2 (TiO modulated with neither facets engineering nor cocatalysts loading), 3HF-TiO 2 (TiO 2 singly modulated with crystal facets engineering), 0HF-TiO 2 /0.5Cr 2 O 3 (TiO singly modulated with the optimal cocatalysts loading) and 2HF-TiO 2 /0.2Cr 2 O 3 (TiO 2 co-modulated with crystal facets engineering and cocatalysts loading). Fig. 5. (a) The LSV curves in 1 M NaOH solution under 300 W Xe lamp illumination (100 mW/cm 2 ); (b) Amperometric I-t curves under chopped light irradiation with on/off interval of 30 s at 1.23 V vs. RHE; (c) EIS spectra; and (d) The time-resolved PL spectra of 0HF-TiO 2 , 2HF-TiO 2 , 3HF-TiO 2 , 0HF-TiO 2 /0.5Cr 2 O 3 and 2HF-TiO 2 /0.2Cr 2 O 3 .
